skill.name: expo-deployment
skill.description: Guide Expo app deployment to EAS builds, iOS App Store, Google Play Store, TestFlight, web hosting, and release workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and run Expo deployments across app stores, TestFlight, Google Play, EAS Hosting, and CI workflows.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, including a critical combined-risk heuristic. Review found Markdown deployment documentation rather than executable skill code or malicious intent. Publish with a medium warning because the skill guides real store submissions, external CLI execution, and credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Commands Can Change External Store State
medium_findings.description: The external command detections are documentation examples for EAS, npm, and TestFlight commands, not hidden code execution. They still deserve a warning because running them can build, submit, deploy, or change production release state for Expo apps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and are intended deployment operations. Confidence is high that they are legitimate examples, but they can affect real external services if executed.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Requires User Caution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Apple API key paths, Apple environment variables, Google service account JSON files, and secret creation commands. This is expected for deployment guidance, but mishandling these values could expose store credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly reference credential files, API key paths, and environment variables. The surrounding text includes secure handling guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Metadata Example Fetches Remote Content
medium_findings.description: The metadata reference includes a JavaScript example that fetches translations from an external service before generating App Store metadata. This is a legitimate integration pattern, but users should trust and review the source before automated metadata pushes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is a real fetch example to an external URL, but it uses example.com and appears to demonstrate a normal localization workflow. Risk depends on the endpoint a user substitutes.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography and low-severity reconnaissance static matches do not correspond to cryptographic APIs, obfuscation, or system probing logic. The reviewed files are Markdown guides with product names, metadata examples, and command snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, crypto API calls, obfuscated code, or reconnaissance logic at the representative flagged areas. The static labels appear to come from generic text and documentation context.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined-Risk Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined command, network, and credential patterns, but all reviewed evidence is instructional Markdown. No evidence found of hidden execution, credential exfiltration, malicious endpoints, or prompt injection instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky categories co-occur in documentation because deployment requires commands, services, and credentials. I did not find evidence that the skill itself executes code or sends secrets anywhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Commands in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell commands for npm, EAS build, EAS submit, TestFlight, credentials, versioning, and status checks. These are expected for a deployment skill and should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and easy to verify in the Markdown. The risk is not covert execution, but user-approved execution with production impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File and Environment Variable Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation references service account JSON files, App Store key files, and environment variables for CI/CD. These are normal deployment requirements but require secret hygiene.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are direct and semantically relevant to store submission. The docs also advise secure storage, which lowers malicious concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Fetch in Config Example
dangerous_patterns.description: A JavaScript metadata example fetches localization content from an external URL. Users should only adapt this pattern with trusted endpoints and reviewed content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call is real but demonstrative and uses an example domain. It becomes risky only if users substitute an untrusted or compromised service.

content.value_statement: Expo releases require coordinated builds, credentials, submissions, store metadata, and rollout workflows. This skill provides focused deployment guidance for EAS, TestFlight, App Store Connect, Google Play, and web previews.
actual_capabilities: Explains EAS CLI setup, project initialization, production build profiles, and submit commands for Expo apps.
Guides iOS submission through App Store Connect credentials, TestFlight, app review preparation, and release options.
Guides Android submission through Google Play service accounts, release tracks, staged rollouts, and app signing.
Describes EAS Hosting deployment, pull request previews, native update previews, and release workflow files.
Documents App Store metadata management, localization, ASO fields, review information, and metadata automation.
limitations: It does not deploy an app by itself; users or agents must run the referenced commands in a project.
It does not verify Apple or Google account permissions before submission.
It does not replace store policy review, privacy review, or legal review for production releases.
It assumes the app already has valid Expo configuration and access to required credentials.

use_cases.title: Prepare a First Store Release
use_cases.description: Create build profiles, configure credentials, and submit initial iOS and Android builds through EAS.

use_cases.title: Automate Release Workflows
use_cases.description: Design EAS workflows for tagged releases, pull request previews, web deploys, and store submissions.

use_cases.title: Improve Store Readiness
use_cases.description: Review metadata, release notes, localization, app review details, and rollout choices before launch.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Deployment Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a step-by-step Expo deployment checklist for my app. Include EAS setup, iOS, Android, TestFlight, Google Play internal testing, and web deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Review My EAS Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Expo deployment plan and point out missing EAS build, submit, credential, versioning, and rollout steps before production release.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Store Submissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan iOS App Store and Google Play submissions for an Expo app. Include required accounts, credentials, metadata, testing tracks, and review risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design CI Release Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an EAS workflow strategy for tagged production releases, pull request previews, web deploys, native updates, and staged app store rollouts.

output_examples.input: I need to ship my Expo app to TestFlight first.
output: A readiness checklist for App Store Connect, bundle identifiers, Apple credentials, version numbers, and TestFlight tester groups.
A recommended sequence for building, submitting, checking status, and collecting tester feedback before App Store review.

output_examples.input: I want Google Play internal testing before production.
output: A setup plan covering Play Console app creation, service account permissions, secure key storage, internal track configuration, and submission monitoring.
A release path from internal testing to staged production rollout with version code checks.

output_examples.input: I want deployment automation for pull requests and releases.
output: A workflow outline for web previews on pull requests, native update previews, tagged production builds, and store submissions.
Notes about keeping secrets in managed secret storage and reviewing custom run steps before enabling automation.

best_practices: Run TestFlight or internal Play testing before production release.
Store Apple and Google credentials in secure secret storage, not in source control.
Use staged rollouts and monitor submission status before expanding release availability.
anti_patterns: Do not commit service account JSON files, App Store keys, or generated credential files.
Do not submit directly to production without tester validation and store metadata review.
Do not copy workflow commands into CI without checking secret scope and account permissions.

faq.question: Does this skill run deployment commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides deployment guidance and command examples that a user or agent may choose to run.

faq.question: Which Expo services does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers EAS Build, EAS Submit, EAS Hosting, EAS Metadata, EAS Update previews, and EAS Workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with both iOS and Android releases?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes App Store Connect, TestFlight, Google Play Console, release tracks, and store submission guidance.

faq.question: Does it manage credentials securely for me?
faq.answer: No. It explains credential options, but users remain responsible for secret storage and access controls.

faq.question: Can it help with app store metadata?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains EAS Metadata, ASO fields, localization, review details, release notes, and common metadata issues.

faq.question: Is this only for production releases?
faq.answer: No. It also covers TestFlight, internal testing, pull request previews, and web deployment previews.
